perturbation.original_text: Either party wishing to terminate the Agreement must give written notice to the other party at least thirty (30) days prior to the desired date of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party wishing to terminate the Agreement must give written notice to the other party at least ten (10) days prior to the desired date of termination.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days could be problematic if state laws require a longer notice period for contract termination in certain situations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey law regarding contract termination requires reasonable notice, which may be interpreted as longer than 10 days depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.J. Stat. § 12A:2-309 requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. Ten (10) days is likely to be found unreasonable.

Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 12A:2-309 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 12A:2-309. Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract, reducing the termination notice period from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this may violate N.J. Stat. § 12A:2-309, which requires 'reasonable notification'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing N.J. Rev. Stat. § 12A:2-309 (2022), states that termination requires 'reasonable notification'. The reduction to 10 days could be deemed unreasonable, creating a contradiction with the legal requirement for reasonable notification. The definition of the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction perturbation type describes how a deadline set in a contract can violate state law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall not be assigned by mPhase without the prior written consent of Lucent.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall not be assigned by mPhase under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Completely prohibiting assignment might conflict with New Jersey law, which generally allows for assignment of contracts unless explicitly prohibited by statute or public policy. An absolute prohibition could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey's policy favoring free assignability of contracts, unless restricted by statute or public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: New Jersey Statute 2A:25-1 generally permits the assignment of contracts. The modification creates an absolute prohibition on assignment by mPhase, which may be challenged if it unreasonably restricts mPhase's rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 2A:25-1 (2022) - Contracts and judgments assignable; action by assignee; defenses. :: 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 N.J. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 2A - Administration of Civil and Criminal Justice Section 2A:25-1 - Contracts and judgments assignable; action by assignee; defenses. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 2A:25-1 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 2A:25-1 Contracts and judgments assignable; action by assignee; defenses. 2A:25-1. All contracts for the sale and conveyance of real estate, all judgments and decrees recovered in any of the courts of this State or of the United States or in any of the courts of any other state of the United States and all choses in action arising on contract shall be assignable, and the assignee may sue thereon in his own name. In such an action, the person sued shall be allowed, not only all set-offs, discounts and defenses he has against the assignee, but also all set-offs, discounts and defenses he had against the assignor before notice of such assignment was given to him. The assignment of a sealed instrument by writing not under seal shall be as valid as if under seal. The assignee for a valuable consideration of any chose in action may, although the assignor is dead, sue for and recover the same in his own name. The person sued in any such action shall be allowed not only all set-offs, discounts and defenses he has against the assignee, but also all set-offs, discounts and defenses he had against the assignor or his representatives before notice of such assignment was given to him. Security interests in commercial tort claims may be created, attached, perfected and enf
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an absolute prohibition on assignment, while New Jersey Statute 2A:25-1, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally permits the assignment of contracts. The original text allowed assignment with consent, but the change removes this possibility entirely. This complete removal contradicts the general assignability permitted by law, potentially restricting mPhase's rights unreasonably. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' applies as the contract sets a requirement that likely violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California, even though both parties are based in New Jersey, and Connecticut, might conflict with established principles of contract law regarding choice of law clauses, particularly if California has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. This could be seen as an attempt to circumvent New Jersey's or Connecticut's specific laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of law principles requiring a reasonable relationship to the chosen jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law of the state chosen by the parties will be applied unless the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Because the parties are in New Jersey and Connecticut, California law would not apply and will be contradictory to this section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from New York to California. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 indicates the chosen state's law applies only if it has a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction, or there's a reasonable basis for the choice, and it doesn't contradict a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. Given the parties are based in New Jersey and Connecticut, and the Perturbation Explanation suggests this change might circumvent those states' laws, California law's application could be deemed contradictory. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this by stating that conflict of laws arises when a difference exists between jurisdictions, and a court must determine which law to apply. The change directly contradicts the established legal principles regarding choice of law, making it a significant contradiction.
